So welcome back to module 9 of Straight Line Persuasion. Now you're really in the home stretch, only one more module to go after this one. And this particular module, very powerful. What you're going to learn here is two very, very important things. One are trigger words that actually bypass the conscious mind and allow you to influence and persuade on an unconscious level. These words might seem ridiculous at first hand, but here's the deal. It's scientifically proven. There's been studies, and they've actually gone through, you know, double blind studies where they've shown now a word like only or a word like because can dramatically increase your ability to persuade somebody to do something. Secondly, you're going to learn what's called offsetting language patterns. And what offsetting language patterns are, are phrases and groups of words that create a specific emotion that neutralizes a negative emotion. So if someone might be worried, then you could create a language pattern that offsets worry. If someone wants to think about it because they think it might be too expensive or they're just simply one of those people that wants to think about it, you're going to learn a language pattern that will offset someone's notion that thinking about things actually makes any sense. And I'll tell you this, and all the objections I've ever gotten as a salesperson, I want to think about it as one that, you know, I hate, you know, I want to smack someone, right? Because, you know, what does that really mean? So offsetting language patterns here are going to really take you from, you know, a level eight as a persuader, as a straight line persuader to level 10. It's going to allow you to loop in a way that is really, really elegant and really, really effective. So go at this one, you know, with some gusto. You got one more left after this. Take the action book out and write down the offsetting language patterns, write them down and memorize these. Take these to heart and also develop some of your own based on your specific industry. You'll see many of the patterns I give you for language are generic because I wanted to keep it broad. So people from all walks of life, you know, people that are not even really selling in the traditional sense, might be selling information, might be just trying to sell their vision, might be a teacher looking to get her kids to do their homework, right? You know, it could be anything. But these language patterns and these key trigger words are going to propel you to a whole new level and they're going to make you sound what I call good. You know, you see someone, wow, he sounds really good or she sounds really good. So that's what these keywords will help you do and that's what these language patterns will help you accomplish. So go at it and I'll see you real soon. There are certain words that we're going to use here. Okay, let me give you these words. They're called trigger words. They devilish these trigger words, virtually. Okay. Virtually is a great hedge word for legality. Very few things in life happen always, but a lot of things happen virtually always. Use virtually whenever you can to not put yourself in a regulatory box. Do not speak in absolutes. I promise you this, you know, all my clients have made money. Virtually all my clients have gotten good results. Not all my clients have gotten good results. It is a very important word when you're selling. When you make guarantees to people, they kind of know it's bullshit anyway. Every one of my clients makes money. Oh, really? Come on, stop. Most of my clients, that's more reasonable. Virtually all my clients, they know it, but what happens is virtually is sort of a weird word. It's short circuits there. They know it. They're like, they know you hedged. They get it. They know you've hedged somehow. They're not really sure how, but you get the point across that what you have is really, really cool. You could say most of my clients have made money or virtually all my clients, they virtually get wins the ticket every time. A much more powerful word. Next word. Only. This is huge. Only is a minimizer. That means when you ask for an order and you put a dollar amount, you don't just say, it's $49.95. I see a direct marketing whiz back there saying, yeah, this is obvious stuff, right? The minimizes the impact of the price. They've done studies with this stuff. It's wild. I'll tell you about a couple of stories in a second. Next, because. Justifier. Now if you think, how powerful can be caused be? Let me tell you how powerful was because of me. There's a guy named Robert Cildini who wrote a book. He was about influence. He's a teacher, a professor. He wrote this amazing book on influence. It's the science of influence. He actually did studies and he tested this stuff. Here's one of the tests that he did. In college, I think it was in Arizona, I think, the Arizona state. They sat by a copy machine. That was a busy copy machine. They waited until there was like seven or eight people on line. Then somebody would just walk up to the front of line and say, can I cut the line and make my copies? About 25% of the time, the whole line would say, yeah, whatever. They let the person make copies. Right? 25%. They went back the next day with a new strategy. Same line. You might have cut the line because I'm in a rush. 75% said, yeah, go ahead, cut the line. Because I'm in a rush. Because I'm in a rush. You're saying, well, okay, it's because he's in a rush. Uh-uh. He cut it again. Excuse me. You mind if I cut the line because I really need to? Okay. 75% said, yes. Look it up. It's true. Because I need to. It doesn't matter what the because even is. Just it doesn't matter what it is. It can be a because or any. Of course, the sky is freaking blue. Okay. For some reason, it flips a switch in us. That's just, well, okay. He's got a reason. He's justified. He's a justifier. They did the same thing with the word only. Very similar thing. They said, you need to cut the line because I'm in a rush. It'll only take a minute. 95% of the people say cut the line. They added only. This works. Don't kid yourself. You use only when you ask for price, when you ask for commitments, use the word because to justify things you ask for, why you need them. It doesn't really matter what you say. But why not say something smart? Not because, you know, my mom is in a rush to get on the high. I'm gonna say something smart, right? Hugely powerful words. Here's another one to show you this human psychology. Let's talk about reciprocity. Giving value first and then getting massive value back from somebody else. We'll get into this a bit later in the end of the presentation with developing customers for life. But the idea of reciprocity is simple. You give and people feel obligated. It's one of the cornerstones of seven principles of sales, scientifically reciprocity. You did a study with a waitress. They actually tested to see how you could increase the tip as a waitress. So the waitress goes over and she gives you the check. You get X%, 10% tip. Waitress comes over and she gives you the tip with the smiley face that she draws in front of her, in front on the check. Smiley face, thank you. She gets 15%. Then she drops two chocolates with the check at the table and she gets 25%. Again, for the chocolates. You get more money for the chocolates. Very good, right? Here's the clincher. The waitress comes over. She puts the smiley face on the check. She drops the chocolates on the check. She gives them the check. She walks away, turns around and puts three more chocolates on. 75% tip. Get it? You're special. Is this special? You're not good people. Here, take some extra. 75% of the testiners. I'm not making this. I'm going to look it up on the internet. It's true. I couldn't believe it myself. I'm like, that's how crazy it is out there. If I'd been your advisor, I'd been your sales rep, I'd been selling you flowers, I'd been selling you cars, if you'd been booking seminars with me, if you'd been buying printing supplies from me, if you'd been booking air travel with me, I'd been putting you on the best flights and the best cruises and cruises. It's for any business, not just stocks, bonds, anything, anything. I'd been your advisor for the last three or four years, showing you consistent performance, making you money on a consistent basis, saving you money on a consistent basis, delivering you a product on a consistent basis, making sure your clients are satisfied on a consistent basis. You wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. You'd be saying, go ahead and do it. Am I right? I didn't use the right language pattern there. Just like tonality, I just was kidding around. You would say, do it. You'd be very nice. You'd be very proud if you were saying do it. That's the real pattern. Don't ever forget tonality. That's your pattern for closing, tonal pattern. Please, don't be so serious. Please, please, please use this stuff ethically. I'm going to say it again. All right. It's very powerful. Use it to help people make good decisions to buy things they should be buying and power people to break through barriers and have been holding them back for making good decisions. That's what this is for. It's not to get people and extract money out of their wallet to put into yours. Shame on you if you do that. I did it and went to jail. Was separated from my children. I lost all my money, my self-respect. The fact that I'm standing here is a miracle. I'm one of the few that can bounce back. Mostly people don't. So learn from my mistakes. Use this stuff and it works, boy. You know it works, it works, but use it right. And you will soar. You will soar with it. So remember the key with offsetting language patterns and trigger words is that you have to use them at specific times that make sense in your industry. I want you now as an exercise to identify two of the language patterns that I laid out for you. I want you to then customize them so they directly suit your specific field. So if you're a stock broker, you wouldn't add a stock into that. It might be something about a certain share, trading it at some price and what's the worst that can possibly happen. We can work with a stop loss. If you're a real estate broker, it might be something about what's the worst that can happen. Let's say you put a down payment on the house and you can't get a mortgage. You could always get the money back. Whatever it might be, there's a language pattern that disarms someone and prevents them from getting into that I wanna think about it thing. It also can minimize the sort of impact that a big number might have. Let's say a product might cost $50,000. Well, saying it costs $50,000 versus being a cash outlay of $50,000 or better yet, a cash outlay of only $50,000, it becomes infinitely more palatable to your prospect. So again, do the exercise. Take two key language patterns, customize them to your industry and tomorrow I want you to use them.
